name: Motivational Interviewing
tagline: Resolve your own ambivalence about change by listening to yourself.
situations: Torn between changing and staying the same
Knowing you 'should' change but not wanting to
Stuck in a habit you're of two minds about
Pressured by others and digging in
summary: Motivational Interviewing (MI) is a way of resolving the ambivalence that keeps us stuck. Rather than arguing yourself into change (which usually triggers resistance), you draw out your own reasons for it. Adapted as a self-reflection, MI helps you tip your internal balance toward change by listening to the part of you that already wants it.
howItWorks: Ambivalence is normal: part of you wants to change and part wants to stay the same. Pushing hard on the 'should' side provokes the other side to defend the status quo — we believe what we hear ourselves argue for. MI works by deliberately eliciting and strengthening your own 'change talk' (your reasons, desire, and confidence for change) instead of the counter-arguments. Hearing yourself voice why and how you'd change builds genuine, self-generated motivation.

steps.instruction: Name the change you're ambivalent about, without judging either side.
List the good things about staying the same — honestly. Ambivalence is real.
Then ask yourself the change questions: Why might I want this? How would my life be better? What gives me confidence I could?
steps.note: Spend most of your time and energy here.

steps.instruction: Rate your importance and confidence 0–10, then ask 'why that number and not lower?' to surface more change talk.

whenToUse: When you're genuinely torn about a change
When 'shoulds' from others make you dig in
Stuck in a habit you're ambivalent about
Before committing to a hard behavior change
whenNotToUse: When you've already decided — then move to planning (implementation intentions)
To pressure or trick yourself past real reservations
For changes driven purely by others' expectations, not your values
tips: Spend more time on change talk than on the pros of staying
Ask 'why not a lower number?' to draw out your own reasons
Be curious, not coercive, with yourself
Follow a resolved 'yes' with a concrete first step

evidence.summary: Motivational Interviewing has extensive meta-analytic support across addictions, health behavior change, and more; eliciting change talk predicts behavior change.
citations: Miller, W.R. & Rollnick, S. (1991). Motivational Interviewing. Guilford Press.
Lundahl, B. et al. (2010). A meta-analysis of MI. Research on Social Work Practice.
